biographySource.title: Smithsonian American Art Museum: George Caleb Bingham

artworks.title: The Jolly Flatboatmen
artworks.description: A flatboat crew pauses work to dance and play music, framing river labor as social life.
artworks.rights: NGA Open Access / public domain image availability noted on NGA object page.
artworks.creditLine: National Gallery of Art (Open Access image via Wikimedia Commons)
context: Bingham presents transport labor as a public and performative culture.
The river is treated as economic infrastructure before rail dominance.
The painting became one of his most recognized national works.

artworks.title: Cottage Scenery
artworks.description: A domestic frontier setting within a carefully observed landscape.
artworks.rights: NGA Open Access / public domain image availability.
artworks.creditLine: National Gallery of Art (image via Wikimedia Commons)
context: The composition emphasizes settled land instead of wilderness spectacle.
Small figures help scale the natural environment.
The painting aligns with mid-19th-century rural identity building.

artworks.title: Fur Traders Descending the Missouri
artworks.description: Two traders and a bear cub drift downstream in a tightly framed river scene.
artworks.rights: Public domain artwork; metadata from The Met collection page.
artworks.creditLine: The Metropolitan Museum of Art (public domain image via Wikimedia Commons)
context: The painting condenses commerce, travel, and display into one river vignette.
Its intimate scale contrasts with Bingham's larger civic compositions.
The river functions as a trade corridor and social stage.

artworks.title: The County Election
artworks.description: A dense polling scene where participation, pressure, and spectacle overlap.
artworks.rights: SLAM object page identifies the work as public domain.
artworks.creditLine: Saint Louis Art Museum (image via Wikimedia Commons)
context: This work is part of Bingham's Election Series.
The composition shows competing visions of civic order.
Bingham records political behavior across social groups.

artworks.title: Stump Speaking
artworks.description: A candidate addresses a mixed audience, turning politics into staged public argument.
artworks.rights: SLAM object page identifies the work as public domain.
artworks.creditLine: Saint Louis Art Museum (image via Wikimedia Commons)
context: Public speech is presented as both persuasion and performance.
Bingham draws on Missouri campaign culture.
Audience reactions become central narrative evidence.

artworks.title: The Verdict of the People
artworks.description: Crowds process election results outside a courthouse through celebration and conflict.
artworks.rights: SLAM object page identifies the work as public domain.
artworks.creditLine: Saint Louis Art Museum (image via Wikimedia Commons)
context: The painting completes the Election Series.
Voting outcomes are staged as communal spectacle.
Non-voting observers remain visible in the political scene.

artworks.title: Raftsmen Playing Cards
artworks.description: Raftsmen pause for cards and conversation while drifting through calm water.
artworks.rights: Public domain reproduction listed on Wikimedia Commons.
context: Bingham repeatedly contrasts labor with social downtime.
Card play reflects risk culture in river economies.
The scene keeps attention on working-class recreation.

artworks.title: The Wood-Boat
artworks.description: Men transfer and sell firewood for steamboat fuel in a river logistics scene.
artworks.rights: Public domain reproduction listed on Wikimedia Commons.
artworks.creditLine: Saint Louis Art Museum (image via Wikimedia Commons)
context: The painting foregrounds supply chains behind river transport.
Bingham documents infrastructure rather than only scenery.
Foreground figures anchor broader market exchange.

artworks.title: Mississippi Boatman
artworks.description: A single river worker is rendered with portrait-level attention.
artworks.rights: Public domain reproduction listed on Wikimedia Commons.
context: Bingham combines genre painting and portrait conventions.
The figure stands for occupational identity on inland rivers.
The work isolates character rather than crowd narrative.

artworks.title: Landscape with Cattle
artworks.description: Pastoral terrain and livestock connect regional identity to agricultural production.
artworks.rights: Public domain reproduction listed on Wikimedia Commons.
artworks.creditLine: Saint Louis Art Museum (image via Wikimedia Commons)
context: Landscape and labor economies remain linked through livestock imagery.
The work complements Bingham's civic and river scenes.
Managed land becomes a core marker of regional identity.

artworks.title: The Storm
artworks.description: Darkened sky and shifting light turn landscape into a scene of instability.
artworks.rights: Public domain reproduction listed on Wikimedia Commons.
context: The scene departs from electoral narratives toward atmospheric drama.
Weather is used as narrative pressure rather than backdrop.
It demonstrates range across Bingham's thematic practice.

artworks.title: Country Politician
artworks.institution: Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco
artworks.description: An early political scene centered on persuasion and local party dynamics.
artworks.rights: Public domain reproduction listed on Wikimedia Commons.
artworks.creditLine: Google Art Project image via Wikimedia Commons
context: This political subject predates the Election Series panels.
Conversation and gesture drive the narrative.
Bingham tracks electoral culture beyond formal voting scenes.
